author: @aaronking8706
text:
Just the other week I got incredible results from a makeup mirror.  10 x magnification with a focal length of 760mm. Using a magnifying glass and an old mobile phone set up within the FL, I got some nice digital photos at 10x * 2x = 20x magnification maybe. The makeup mirrors are super cheap at any big box store.
Just waiting for the clouds to go away and the moon to be in my slither of sky.

author: @whosle
text: Cool development. Are you going to try and make a concave lens? Perhaps try freezing the water and hoping for super clear ice?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - the lens is only convex because the curve points out - the lenses are two formed surfaces if we flip the surface around then they would be concave - so the construction is basically the same - to prevent pressure from the water 'popping out' the lens a thicker plastic sheet would be needed but I would think that would really be all that is needed

replies.author: @whosle
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Ah right, the pressure would just push it out with thin plastic. Lots of potential applications for it !

author: @saiello2061
text: I used to grind and figure my own newtonian telescope mirrors, a lot of work. Be interesting to see how effective this would be on the moon and other celestial objects with a x2 focal length objective and x1/4 sized eyepiece. Should give you the same magnification as a high powered binocular ( ~x16 ). The focal lengths of first telescope objectives were idiotically long to help combat the inherent chromatic and spherical abberations in a single piece glass lens but they made some amazing discoveries nonetheless! But hey, with this method, if you can get to see craters on the Moon which you wouldn't otherwise be able see then that would be excellent...! 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well worth the effort mate

author: @actudoran
text:
Haha
Cool!
Can you make a crown and flint setup for the objective? 
Super stuff!  I got to admit I didn't think you'd be using the same diameter for the objective as for the occular :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it was just quicker to do mate - better would have been a 1/4 objective - but for me that would have meant another blowing box and as you know I tend to do POC quickly and cheaply - just to see if it works - after that I would do it again with what I have learnt and spend more time and care

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering i understand completely! Does the job for demonstrative purposes.
Huge eye relief OTOH :)
Could be placed in the same dimensions boards and make an almost cube like telescope with the smaller lens for an occular :)

I wanted to 3d print the scope I designed but never got to it as the most expensive parts had to be purchased anyway hence the cost savings were inexistent ... For example a midrange focuser would cost close to the price of the complete optical tube ... Made more sense to buy 2nd hand equipment and upgrade the weakest points. The goal is NEO detection so high accuracy requirements ment no room for "should work" ... 
I've since altered the design and will make a pure astrograph with two different focal length on the same tube assembly to be used alternatively.
Point is to work out the mechanism of switching between configs sans screwing the collimation at all.

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: @actudoran mate you clearly know what you are doing - and I clearly don't - yet lol - it would be awesome to see you tackle this with water lenses - a high power telescope at pennies - that would be fantastic

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text:
@TnTOmnibus making something high power ... not that hard, making it okish could be tricky ... 
had a look the other day at the refractive indexes of various liquids to use as a combo of say water and glycol ... like say have a double convex lens filled with water like Rob's and to the back of it just a straight plain sheet of plastic where you fill it with glycol ... problem is there will be a series of mediums for the light to go through: plastic film, water, plastic film, glycol, plastic film ... it just turns the whole thing into a bit of a nightmare for calculating the change of the total system with temperature variations ... :)

Never mind me ... I am used to all those issues and gave in ... about a year ago I dished out the cash for a triplet apochromatic beast ... 

OTOH termoformed concave surface to be mirrored with silver nitrate could make a nice little mirror :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate
